title: Hypothetical number of deaths from energy production
subtitle: Hypothetical number of global deaths which would have resulted from energy production if the world's energy production was met through a single source, in 2014. This was assumed based on energy production death rates and IEA estimates of global energy consumption in 2014 of 159,000 [terawatt-hours](#dod:watt-hours).
caption:
Hypothetical number of deaths from energy production

Hypothetical number of global deaths which would have resulted from energy production if the world's energy production was met through a single source, in 2014. This was assumed based on energy production death rates and IEA estimates of global energy consumption in 2014 of 159,000 terawatt-hours.

This horizontal bar chart displays the hypothetical number of global deaths that would have occurred in 2014 if the world's total energy consumption of 159,000 terawatt-hours was generated entirely by a single energy source. The data is arranged in descending order of mortality impact. Brown coal represents the highest value, with a bar extending to 5.2 million deaths. Coal follows as the second highest, reaching 3.91 million deaths. Oil occupies the third position with a value of 2.93 million deaths. These three fossil fuel sources form a distinct upper cluster, with all values exceeding 2.9 million. Below this group, the values drop significantly into the hundreds of thousands. Biomass is recorded at 736,170 deaths, followed by Gas at 448,539 deaths. Nuclear energy sits at the bottom of the chart with the lowest value, showing a bar that corresponds to 11,766 deaths. The visual gap between the top three categories and the bottom three is substantial, highlighting the difference in scale between the millions of deaths associated with coal and oil versus the thousands associated with nuclear energy under these specific hypothetical conditions.
